sources.context: blameless accountability frameworks
Scrum empirical pillar
the Driving-archetype topology that merges directing/doing/delivering into one holistic unit; goal is adaptiveness and customer-centricity
Appreciative Interviews LS pattern
a named pattern for how an organisational unit is structured and operates; 16 archetypes arranged in four groups on the OT map
Lean Startup cycle
AAR facilitation principle
change fails when people don't own the ideas; MADE method as org-design-driven change path
co-creation as both the Primer's own production principle and an emergent quality that scales with rightward and upward movement on the OT map
complete archetypes deliver customer value independently; incomplete archetypes require other archetypes to collectively complete delivery

FLA teams must guard against making counterfactual arguments; learn why people did what they actually did, not why they did not do something hindsight suggests they should have (Part 9, Avoid Counterfactual Arguments)
Directing + Delivering topology optimised for output predictability; risks feature bloat when discovery remains separated from delivery
AAR as discovery learning vehicle
Scrum empirical process control
moving an org design toward upper-right on the OT map by expanding skills mandate and work mandate; AI makes elevation easier
common consequence of Delivery Topology output focus when discovery is separated from delivery; no guarantee output solves real problems
org design goal aligned with business objective so that each element reinforces the others; the test for strategic org design
flow as the design goal of rightward movement on the skills-mandate axis; local optimisation degrades system flow
contrast with evidence-based practice
just culture decision tree branches
the anti-pattern of adopting an industry-standard management framework as substitute for strategic org design; yields root-cause neglect and low ownership
Scrum empirical pillar
emergence through improvisation
AAR leader-led vs. external facilitator
four-step org change process: Map current topology, Assess strategic alignment, Design target topology, Elevate using katas
discovery-delivery loop framework
shared discourse using short archetype names (CAPS-2, WHOLE-3) to describe current and target org state without framework-specific jargon
the mapping framework: two axes (skills mandate, work mandate), 16 archetypes in four groups, three topologies; the primary source
strategic org design as alternative to canned framework adoption; MADE method as the change path
org structure as deliberate design across skills-mandate and work-mandate axes; topology as stable structural region
participatory ownership over compliance
archetypes classified as output- or outcome-oriented; more output does not mean more outcomes and can lower profit
people must own change ideas to commit fully; OT is explicitly human-centric on this principle; the basis for co-creation over canned solutions
Directing + Doing topology optimised for resource utilisation; fit for temporary relationships but limits learning to skill refinement only
Kanban core value
corrective training outcome of AAR
Scrum team self-organisation
the horizontal OT axis: how broad a unit's skill mandate is; increases from Functional to Unbounded; determines transaction costs and flow
Donaldson and Preston taxonomy: descriptive, instrumental, normative; Ch 3.2 endorses the normative basis
Corporation has obligation to groups beyond shareholders; public benefit corporation as alternative legal structure (Ch 13.2)
Freeman's stakeholder theory: corporations should treat all constituencies fairly; framed alongside CSR triple bottom line
2025-edition concept: focus AI investment in elevation-relevant areas; expertise scarcity vanishes with intelligence-as-a-service; Driving archetypes become more feasible
aligning business strategy, organisational goals, and org design elements so each reinforces the others to be fit for purpose
systems thinking as MAP-step discipline against local optimisation of isolated org-design elements
the super-unit structure at highest vertical levels in the Adaptive Topology where units co-own work with overlapping scope
Scrum empirical pillar
source domain for learning review methodology
the vertical OT axis: how broad a unit's field of play is; increases from Tasks to Unbounded; determines switching costs and strategic direction
sprint planning tool
practices and principles for the ELEVATE step; thoughtful experiments that change structure, processes, and policies toward a target topology

ooda-loop.name: OODA loop (Boyd's *Patterns of Conflict*, credited at MCDP-1 Ch 2 Notes 18)

commanders-intent.name: Commander's intent (MCDP-1 Ch 4) — senior gives what and why, never how

intent-two-levels-up.name: Intent must be understood two levels up (MCDP-1 Ch 4)

schwerpunkt-main-effort.name: Schwerpunkt / main effort (MCDP-1 Ch 4) — one unit / one objective is *the* decisive bid

surfaces-and-gaps.name: Surfaces and gaps (MCDP-1 Ch 2) — avoid strength, exploit weakness

reconnaissance-pull-vs-command-push.name: Reconnaissance pull vs command push (Lind 1985, cited MCDP-1 Ch 4 Notes 11)

combined-arms-dilemma.name: Combined arms — pose the enemy with a dilemma, not a problem (MCDP-1 Ch 2)

doctrine-as-way-of-thinking.name: Doctrine is a way of thinking, not a checklist (MCDP-1 Ch 3)

leniency-overbold-severity-inaction.name: Leniency on overbold errors, severity on errors of inaction (MCDP-1 Ch 3)

topsight-gelernter.name: Topsight (Gelernter's *Mirror Worlds*, 1991, cited MCDP-1 Ch 4 Notes 9)

friction-clausewitz.name: Friction (Clausewitz, central to MCDP-1's understanding of war)

human-dimension-of-war.name: Human dimension of war (MCDP-1 Ch 1) — *no degree of technological development will diminish it*

zweikampf-violent-struggle.name: Zweikampf — war as violent struggle between two hostile, independent, irreconcilable wills (MCDP-1 Ch 1)

boyd-lineage-mcdp1.name: Boyd → Lind → Wyly → Wilson intellectual lineage of MCDP-1

us-government-public-domain-doctrine.name: US Government public-domain doctrine — freely distributable
